data.content: The bug is clear — `ProcessPayment` only checks `amount == 0` but not negative values. The fastest workflow is `dotnet watch test`, which auto-reruns tests on every file save. Let me set that up:

toolRequests.name: bash

arguments.description: Start dotnet watch test for the failing test

toolRequests.intentionSummary: Start dotnet watch test for the failing test

data.reasoningText:
The bug is clear: `ProcessPayment` only checks for zero but not negative amounts. The fix is to add a check for negative amounts. 

The user asked about the fastest workflow for iterating. The answer is `dotnet watch test` which will re-run tests automatically on file save. Let me start that up and then fix the bug.

arguments.description: Start dotnet watch test for the failing test

toolCalls.name: bash
toolCalls.args: {"command": "cd /tmp/sv-f06c267cf96e4c12b0e0f2fd43c90169 && dotnet watch test --filter \"ProcessPayment_NegativeAmount_ThrowsException\" 2>&1", "description": "Start dotnet watch test for the failing test", "mode": "async", "shellId": "watch-test"}

toolArgs.description: Start dotnet watch test for the failing test

data.content: Good — `dotnet watch` is running and waiting for file changes. There are two issues: a missing `using System;` and the missing negative amount validation. Let me fix both:

arguments.old_str:
if (amount == 0) throw new ArgumentException("Amount cannot be zero");
        // Missing: validation for negative amounts
arguments.new_str: if (amount <= 0) throw new ArgumentException("Amount must be positive");

data.reasoningText: There's a missing `using System;` directive. Let me fix that and the actual bug (negative amount check) in one go. The watch will automatically pick up the changes.

arguments.old_str:
if (amount == 0) throw new ArgumentException("Amount cannot be zero");
        // Missing: validation for negative amounts
arguments.new_str: if (amount <= 0) throw new ArgumentException("Amount must be positive");
